title: Spirit Airlines and the Death of Leisure for the Non-Leisure Class
desc: The low-cost carrier was a mess. But it was also an icon of budget travel, facilitating a kind of modest freedom for the masses.

title: Olivier Assayas’s Coming of Political Age
desc: The director—whose newest film, “The Wizard of the Kremlin,” examines the ascent of Vladimir Putin—discusses a few of the books that have helped to shape his ideals.

title: The Best Books of 2026 So Far
desc: Our editors and critics review notable new fiction, nonfiction, and poetry.

title: The Hollow Trickery of “The Wizard of the Kremlin
desc: Olivier Assayas’s adaptation of a novel about a fictionalized adviser to Vladimir Putin reduces politics to personalities and atrocities to anecdotes.

title: What “The Sheep Detectives” Doesn’t Understand About Sheep
desc: The new film, starring Hugh Jackman and Emma Thompson, is based on a near-perfect “sheep crime novel”—but the adaptation shows disappointingly little interest in the animal mind.

title: A Tree Grows in Marburg in “Silent Friend
desc: In Ildikó Enyedi’s meditative nature epic, three lonely experimenters from three different eras seek to unlock the secrets of plants—and learn something vital about themselves.

title: The Furious Moral Clarity of Lucrecia Martel
desc: In the Argentinean filmmaker’s new documentary, “Our Land,” and a recently restored masterpiece, “The Headless Woman,” an elusiveness of form becomes the most direct way to the truth.

title: Restaurant Review: Lysée
desc: One evening a week, at Eunji Lee’s tiny Manhattan pâtisserie, Lysée, sweets are appetizer, entrée, and everything else.

title: Restaurant Review: Vato and Los Burritos Juarez
desc: The slim, tortilla-forward northern-Mexican burrito is getting its due, at spots including Vato, in Park Slope, and Los Burritos Juarez, in Fort Greene.

title: Half Man” TV
desc: Richard Gadd’s follow-up to “Baby Reindeer” traces a decades-long quasi-familial relationship that’s thornier than any other male bond on TV.

title: Euphoria” ’s Descent Into Hell
desc: With Season 3, the HBO drama feels like it’s clicked into its final, hardened form: a thrilling, disturbing horror show, delivered with a sneer and a smile.

title: Big Mistakes” Is a Crime Show for the Girls and the Gays
desc: Dan Levy’s first scripted series since “Schitt’s Creek” is another fish-out-of-water comedy—this one set in a very different milieu.

title: Sharp Claws at “Becky Shaw” and “Cats: The Jellicle Ball
desc: Gina Gionfriddo’s zinger-filled sex farce and the celebratory ballroom-culture adaptation of Andrew Lloyd Webber’s confounding musical are cathartic catnip.

title: Dog Day Afternoon” on Broadway, Reviewed
desc: Sidney Lumet’s kinetic, emotionally complex film has been transformed into a hokey sitcom with gunshots.

title: Rostam Batmanglij Wanders to the Edges of American Sound
desc: The polymath musician, formerly of Vampire Weekend, likes to push our idea of what a pop song can be.

title: The History of Jazz Has Instantly Expanded
desc: Newly released archival live performances by Ahmad Jamal, Joe Henderson, and Cecil Taylor illuminate their legacies and the art form at large.

title: The Art of the Fictional Pop Song
desc: The chart-topping hits you hear in movies can stretch the limits of belief. On the “Mother Mary” soundtrack, Charli XCX and Jack Antonoff capture the real thing.

title: The Surrealist Blues Poet aja monet’s Jazzy New Album
desc: Also: Joan Semmel’s revolutionary nudes, Aleshea Harris’s film adaptation of “Is God Is,” Rachel Syme on thrift markets galore, and more.

title: Music Review: Kacey Musgraves’s “Middle of Nowhere
desc: On her new album, “Middle of Nowhere,” the singer toys with two of country music’s great themes: her home state of Texas, and solitude.
